title: Known-Answer Tests, Negative Vectors, Fuzzing, and Interoperability · Concurrent Code Workshop
summary: A product-security pair incrementally builds layered cryptographic verification harness, changes one attacker-controlled input, and keeps earlier Go, accessibility, secret-lifecycle, testing, and evidence invariants active. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of layered cryptographic verification harness while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use authoritative known-answer and protocol vectors with provenance, exact bytes, expected successes, expected failures, and stable fixtures.
Mutate every authenticated field, key, nonce, tag, length, order, version, context, and boundary and require clean rejection without partial effects.
Define properties such as round-trip within valid domains, tamper rejection, parser totality, uniqueness invariants, and version separation without asserting false universal laws.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Cryptography and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Crypto Go Authoritative Vectors
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop cryptographic engineering team handles case 3538 during a credential migration window. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine authoritative vectors, negative mutation, bounded fuzzing, reproducibility, and cross-implementation evidence; record key custody, terminal failure, rollback, recovery, and residual-risk ownership; then remove one transfer gate and state exactly which production claim is no longer supported. Browser code uses pure state models or the narrow side-effect-free crypto allowlist with fixed fixtures. Production entropy and keys, secret custody, native timing and side channels, networks, TLS, HSM/KMS, certificate systems, FIPS or compliance state, load, faults, restore, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Cryptography and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Crypto Go Authoritative Vectors.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for use authoritative known-answer and protocol vectors with provenance, exact bytes, expected successes, expected failures, and stable fixtures. For Cryptography and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Crypto Go Authoritative Vectors, keep Crypto Go Authoritative Vectors connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

steps.title: Cryptography and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Crypto Go Interoperability Matrix
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: crypto-go-interoperability-matrix-19o793" that uses case 5621 to combine authoritative vectors, negative mutation, bounded fuzzing, reproducibility, and cross-implementation evidence. Return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import cryptographic packages, generate or handle production keys, open sockets, contact TLS, HSM/KMS, certificate systems, compliance services, or external effects, read host state, execute host commands, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with Go 1.26.5 compile, standard crypto APIs, authoritative vectors, fuzz, race, native side-channel measurement, interoperability, controlled network and key-custody systems, load, fault, recovery, compliance, and production gates.
